entry.title: Step-by-Step Guide: Adding Your Domain to Trusted Domains in SAP IAS
entry.content:
When using embedded iframes or overlays in an application, which authenticates against the Identity Authentication service, the error detailed in the screenshot below is displayed when trying to log in via the domain of the overlay.

Possible error messages  are displayed:

  • "Your domain is not trusted. Please contact your system administrator."
  • "Your request failed because the page you have to be redirected to is not trusted. Please contact your system administrator."

To fix issue - Configure Trusted Domains

Kindly ask your SAP IAS Admin to follow the below steps to fix the issue

  1. Add the necessary domain(s) to the IAS tenant's trust settings as described in Configure Trusted Domains
  2. Enable third-party cookies in the browser (check the respective browser's documentation);
  3. Disable "Prevent cross-site tracking" or "Do not track" options in the browser (the terminology may vary; check the respective browser's documentation).

entry.title: Did you know: SAP Support Log Assistant 2.0 Self Service Portal? & Trace errors for your IPS logs
entry.content:
SAP Support Log Assistant 2.0 is a powerful tool designed to streamline the analysis of support-related files such as logs, traces, and configuration files. This tool leverages the expertise of SAP Product Support to provide automated solutions and recommendations for known issues.

Try it now - Support log assistant standalone, self-service tool

SAP Video - Support Log Assistant - Self Service Tool Demo

SAP Knowledge Base Article: 2838708

How It Helps: The Support Log Assistant 2.0 helps users by:

  1. Automated Analysis: It scans text-based files for known issues and provides recommendations based on SAP's extensive knowledge base
  2. Efficient Troubleshooting: By identifying and highlighting important details in the files, it accelerates issue resolution without the need to open a support case
  3. Time-Saving: Reduces the manual effort required for log analysis and solution searches, allowing users to focus on other critical tasks

Benefits and Advantages:

  1. 24/7 Availability: The tool is available round-the-clock, providing expert-level log file analysis at any time
  2. Accelerated Resolution: Speeds up the process of resolving issues by automatically identifying problems and suggesting solutions
  3. PDF Reports: Generates detailed PDF reports of log analysis findings and recommendations, which can be shared with peers to extend best practices
  4. Standalone Access: Users can access the tool as a standalone application via the SAP for Me customer portal
  5. Enhanced Collaboration: Facilitates sharing of analysis results with peers, promoting collaborative problem-solving

Use Cases:

  1. Enterprise Data Management: Analyzing log files related to data management systems to identify and resolve issues quickly
  2. SAP Identity Provisioning Logs : Analyzing provisioning job files downloaded to identify and resolve issues quickly with Source & Target Systems defined or Transformation logic issues
  3. HANA Platform: Troubleshooting HANA database-related problems by scanning configuration and trace files
  4. Business Intelligence: Ensuring the health of BI systems by automatically detecting and addressing log file errors
  5. Big Data: Managing and resolving issues in big data environments through automated log analysis
  6. NW JAVA and NW Core: Supporting NetWeaver Java and Core systems by providing solutions to log file issues.

FAQs for Support Log Assistant

SAP Identity Provisioning Service - Provisioning Logs -  Download Logs to analyze

After uploading the log files, Scanning will start and provide you the initial analysis with list of Alerts which is causing the issue of failed and also provides SAP Note KBA details as well

Summary will provide the graphic representation to Download Analysis Report 

Conclusion: 

The SAP Support Log Assistant is an invaluable tool for customers, Partners & SAP users, offering automated, expert-level analysis of support-related files. Its ability to accelerate issue resolution, save time, and facilitate collaboration makes it a must-have for maintaining a healthy SAP environment.

entry.title: SAP CIS Identity Provisioning Service integration with SAP HANA Cloud, HANA Database
entry.content:
This blog post is dedicated to the functionality available from Q32024 and which was long awaited by a lot of customers.

The Identity Provisioning Service (IPS) integration, a part of SAP Cloud Identity Services, is now available for SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA database (HANA DB) (link to a public HANA Cloud roadmap item and a link IPS “What is new” section).

This integration allows for streamlined automated user provisioning, simplifying the management of user access and roles within the HANA Cloud environment. IPS, as a mechanism for assignments transport, maps assignments coming from user source, such as the Identity Directory, to the one in HANA Cloud DB (like HANA roles). By leveraging this functionality, organizations can enhance their identity management processes directly within their cloud database systems.

In this blog post, we will dwell through detailed guidance on configuring and utilizing this IPS service together with HANA DB.

Prerequisites

IPS features a connector for the HANA DB. This plug-in allows for the management of users and their role assignments. When using the SAP HANA Database as the target system, the Identity Provisioning service will manage all users in the user group(s) where the technical user of the service has the OPERATOR privilege. It is possble to set up the same SAP HANA database as a target multiple times in order to manage different user groups, by defining different technical users for the IPS.

Set up in SAP BTP Cockpit to access IPS as a part of SAP CIS

First of all, you need an access to SAP Cloud Identity Services (CIS) tenant, as IPS is a part of it. Above all, please check if you have already assigned CIS tenant, you can do it by following the steps explained here.

If not available, you would be able to get your productive and test tenant as part of a self-service request in SAP BTP cockpit by following the next procedure described in SAP CIS initial set up guide.

Second of all, you would need to configure SAP HANA Cloud “admin-api-access“ service plan (XSUAA instance) to gain access to the Identity Provisioning Service. For that you must have:

  1. SAP BTP global account with a , including the admin-api-access service plan. If you still do not have an account, please see Getting a Global Account and View and Manage Services from the Service Marketplace.
  2. You must have the Subaccount Administrator role collection for the subaccount. Speak to your SAP BTP global account administrator if you do not have this one.
  3. After that, you need to subscribe to the admin-api-access service plan and create a service binding for it. How to do it, we will review in the procedure below.

Procedure

  1. In SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to the subaccount where you want to create the subscription to the SAP HANA Cloud multi-environment administration tools.
  2. Create a subscription to the SAP HANA Cloud administration API service plan:
    1. In the left pane of the subaccount, click “Instances and Subscription” and Click “Create”.
    2. Under Service, pick “SAP HANA Cloud”.
    3. Under Plan, pick admin-api-access.
    4. Under “Runtime environment”, pick “other” for multi-environment option. 



    5. Click “Next” to continue and enter the configuration parameters as JSON:

 

{ 
"technicalUser": true, 
}

 

"technicalUser": This is a mandatory parameter which configures whether this client is a technical user. A technical user is a special user for whom all scopes are set as authorities. If the parameter is set to true, the client grants (client credentials, client x509) return an access token that includes all scopes.

6. Click “Create” to finish the creation of the instance.

3. Create a service binding:

1. In your subaccount, navigate to the “Services” -> “Instances and Subscriptions” page.

2. Go to the “Instances” tab and locate your instance. Click the *** (Actions Menu) icon of the admin-api-access service instance you created and select “Create Service Binding” (Multi-environment) or “Create Service Key” (Cloud Foundry) depending on the subscription chosen above.

3. In the “New Binding” wizard, choose a name for your binding and provide configuration parameters either by uploading a JSON file or by entering them in the dialog. For our demo scenario, let’s use the next configuration:

 

{
  "credential-type": "binding-secret",
  "hide-secret": true
}

 

For more information on service binding parameters, see Binding Parameters of SAP Authorization and Trust Management Service.

4. You have subscribed to the admin-api-access service plan and created a new binding for the service instance.

1. Retrieve “the client ID”, “client secret” and “url” through the service binding on the “Instances and Subscriptions “page of your subaccount by clicking on the “View Credentials” button.

2. You will need these values to configure the OAuth2TokenServiceURL, 'User' and 'Password' properties when configuring the 'SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database' system in SAP Identity Provisioning Service. You will also need some of this information to configure your technical user in the SAP HANA database.

Set up in SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database (HANADB)

  1. For setup of the HANADB, client-id, certificate and issuer are important that can be obtained using the url retrieved in the above step 4.a. To get the certificate and issuer, you need to visit the  location: {url}/sap/trust/jwt.

Sample response of location: {url}/sap/trust/jwt:

 

{
  "issuer_dn": "OU=JWT, CN=https://<random>.authentication.eu12.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token",
  "subject_dn": "OU=JWT, CN=https://<random>.authentication.eu12.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token",
  "purpose": "JWT",
  "certificate": "-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----<encoded-certificate>==-----END CERTIFICATE-----",
  "issuer": "https://<random>.authentication.eu12.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token"
}

 

2. To successfully configure JWT authentication trust to your XSUAA client must be established. This process requires the XSUAA certificate to be available. For that, we can use our guided JWT provider creation wizard in Cloud Central. You only need to provide the issuer URL of your XSUAA. The wizard will also make suggestions for all names, so you only need to provide a name for the provider. The detailed guide how to use it you can find in the following 2-parts series blog-post.

Alternatively, the next sql commands can be used from below. They contain some placeholders in angle brackets. These placeholders have to be replaced as follows:

  • identity, issuer, certificate:
    • The values are extracted from the previous step. Please note “identity” value is client-id, “issuer” is the same as issuer and “certificate” is the same as certificate.
  • certificateName, pseName, userName, usergroupName, jwtProviderName:
    • The values are only used internally and can be chosen accordingly
    • Suggested values are (replacing PRODUCT by something that identifies your product) 
      • certificateName SCIM_<PRODUCT>_CERTIFICATE 
      • pseName SCIM_<PRODUCT>_PSE 
      • userName SCIM_<PRODUCT>_USER 
      • usergroupName SCIM_<PRODUCT>_GROUP 
      • jwtProviderName SCIM_<PRODUCT>_PROVIDER 

3. Open HC Central SQL console and connect to the HANA database any user having the CREATE USERGROUP (for user group creation) and TRUST ADMIN (for certificate maintenance) system privileges. 

Issue the following sql commands to create (if not done yet with Guided JWT provider creation wizard): 

  • PSE (certificate collection) along with the trusted certificates 
  • JWT provider for your authentication that uses the PSE to verify the validity of JWTs: 

 

create certificate <certificateName> from '<certificate>' 
create pse <pseName> 
alter pse <pseName> add certificate <certificateName> 
create jwt provider with issuer '' claim 'client_id' as external identity claim 'client_id'='' priority 210 
set pse <pseName> purpose jwt for provider <jwtProviderName>

 

4. At this step, we are going to create a new usergroup for IPS -managed users and technical database user IPS/SCIM to manage the users. We also configure JWT authentication for the user in which we map the client-id value from the admin-api-access to the database user. 

In the example below the user is part of the user group DEFAULT but you can choose to add it to any other user group. The technical user is set as an OPERATOR for the new SCIM user group. This can be done through GUI but for completeness, here are the SQL statements that can be used. 

 

create usergroup <usergroupName> 
create user <userName> with identity '<identity>' for jwt provider <jwtProviderName> set usergroup default 
grant usergroup operator on usergroup <usergroupName> to <userName>

 

5. Finally, the new user created in step-4 is assigned the ROLE ADMIN system privilege to be able to grant/revoke roles. You don‘t need to use ROLE ADMIN but can also use role groups if the IPS/SCIM user should only manage a subset of roles. 

 

grant create schema to <userName> 
grant role admin to <userName> 
GRANT CATALOG READ TO <userName>

 

IPS set up for SAP HANA Cloud, HANA DB

  1. First and foremost, please get yourself familiar with IAM reference architecture as a starting point. As per our reference architecture, the strategy is to have the Identity Directory (also a component of SAP CIS) as a user source (even if you use other solutions as Microsoft Entra ID). 
  2. Then, please go to Identity provisioning app to set up HANA Cloud as your “Source” or/and “Target” systems as per your use case. If you want to do identity provisioning from one HANA (source) to another HANA (target), configuration on both HANAs is needed, as with the following parameters.  

Property Name 

Description & Value 

Authentication 

Enter: BasicAuthentication 

hana.cloud.db.instance.type 

Enter: hdb 

hana.cloud.db.instance.id 

Refers to a unique identifier associated with a specific database instance within the SAP HANA Cloud. 

 

OAuth2TokenServiceURL 

Enter the OAuth 2.0 Token Service URL. 

For example: https://demo-hc-3-test.authentication.sap.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token 

Password 

(Credential) Enter the OAuth Client Secret, created for your SAP HANA Database system. 

ProxyType 

Enter: Internet 

Type 

Enter: HTTP 

URL 

Enter the SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database API URL. 

For example: https://api.gateway.orchestration.<cluster>-<datacenter>.hanacloud.ondemand.com 

User 

Enter the OAuth Client Id, created for your SAP HANA Database system. 

Note: Please use the following values for the three parameters as mentioned in the service-binding procedure shown above in step 4.a. (when creating your admin-api-access): 

  1. “OAuth2TokenServiceUrl” as the URL  
  2. “User” as the Clientid 
  3. “Password” as the Clientsecret 

There are few additional optional parameters as shown in the screenshot below that help with debugging. Please refer IPS Documentation for more details on the configuration parameters and transformations. 

3. Trigger the Jobs as per your need: 

Read Job:  

Trigger the read job to provision only the new and updated users from the source to the target. After the read job, new users will be created in target and the state of certain users will be updated depending on the differences. For further information on the read job please refer to the document.  

Resync job:  

This job is specifically used to fix inconsistencies between two systems. It reads full entities from source and updates the entities in target to be in sync with source.  At the end of the sync job both the source and target will have same state in respective to resources - users and Groups(roles) in HANA. Refer the document for further details on sync job. 

The two jobs above are mainly used to manage users. For other jobs please refer to the CIS/IPS documentation: 

  1. Simulate Job (estimates the number of entities that will be created, updated, deleted or skipped)  
  2. Validate Job (verifies the content of the entities) 

4. The output of step-2 would look like following with successful Jobs done: 

With that, you will conclude the provisioning of your users to desired systems.

---

Additional helpful sources:

IAS side: 

What's New for Cloud Identity Services 

SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database (Source) 

SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database (Target) 

SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database (Proxy) 

For Neo environment: https://help.sap.com/docs/identity-provisioning/identity-provisioning/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-database 

the HANA DB beta connector is the one for the HANA DB on prem version.  Among other limitations, this connector is only available for standalone SCI tenants running on the NEO infrastructure. More details you can read on the help page. 

HANA Cloud side: 

Admin guides: 

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud/sap-hana-cloud-administration-guide/user-provisioning-with-identity-provisioning-service (Setup in SAP BTP Cockpit) 
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-database-administration-guide/user-provisioning (Setup In SAP HANA database)   

Security Guide: 

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-database-security-guide/user-administration-tools 
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-database-security-guide/sap-identity-provisioning-service

entry.title: Integrating SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys via SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog post provides a step-by-step guide on integrating SAP Sales Cloud V2 with SAP Emarsys using the SAP Integration Suite. The focus is on setting up a straightforward integration flow (iFlow) to extract contact data from SAP Sales Cloud V2 and create corresponding contacts in SAP Emarsys.

The goal is to demonstrate how to connect both systems, using APIs and middleware to automate data transfer. This serves as a foundation for more complex integrations in the future, making it easier to extend the process with additional functionality. A key aspect of this integration is OpenID Connect (OIDC, OAuth 2.0, JWT) based API authentication, which is required to securely access SAP Emarsys. We will cover how authentication works in this context and how to configure it properly within the iFlow.

 

Prerequisites

Before implementing this iFlow ensure that you have the following:

  • An active SAP Integration Suite tenant with Cloud Integration enabled
  • Access to a SAP Sales Cloud V2 account with API permissions
  • Access to an SAP Emarsys account with API permissions

 

Implementation

Overview of the Integration Flow

Overview of the iFlow between SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys using SAP Integration SuiteThe diagram above provides an overview of the iFlow, which connects SAP Sales Cloud V2 with SAP Emarsys using the Cloud Integration component within the SAP Integration Suite. This process automates retrieving contact data from SAP Sales Cloud V2, processes the information, and transfers it to SAP Emarsys to create corresponding contacts.

  1. Start Timer – Initiates the iFlow execution at predefined intervals.
  2. Set Filter – Applies conditions to select only relevant contacts from SAP Sales Cloud V2.
  3. Get Sales Cloud Contacts – Fetches contact data via the SAP Sales Cloud API.
  4. Extract Sales Cloud Contacts – Processes and structures the extracted data.
  5. Configure SAP Emarsys Request –Prepares the Request and Setting Up OAuth Authentication
  6. Create Contacts in SAP Emarsys – Sends the contact data via an API request to SAP Emarsys.

In the following sections, each step of the iFlow will be explained in detail.

Step 1: Start Timer - Triggering the Integration Flow

Timer Configuration – Scheduling the iFlow to run at regular intervals

The iFlow is initiated by a timer-based trigger, which ensures that contact data is regularly synchronized between SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys.

As shown in the screenshot, the timer is configured using a Simple Schedule, where the iFlow runs every hour between February 27, 2025, at 09:00 UTC and March 6, 2025, at 18:00 UTC.

Step 2: Set Filter - Filtering Relevant Contacts

Configure SAP Sales Cloud V2 Request

In this step, we apply a filter to ensure that only the necessary contacts are fetched from SAP Sales Cloud V2. First, we limit the selection to contacts associated with the United States. Second, we include only those that have been newly created or updated within the last hour.

After adding the timer to trigger the iFlow, the next step is to insert a Content Modifier to define the query parameters for retrieving the relevant contact data.

  1. Add a Content Modifier
  2. Navigate to the Message Header tab.
  3. Create a new header entry:
    • Action: Create
    • Name: CamelHttpQuery (you can choose your own name)
    • Source Type: Expression
    • Source Value: $filter=isContactPersonFor/country eq 'US' and (adminData/createdOn gt '${date:now:yyyy-MM-dd}T${date:now:HH}:00:00Z' or adminData/updatedOn gt '${date:now:yyyy-MM-dd}T${date:now:HH}:00:00Z'})

The filter dynamically adjusts the timestamp to the beginning of the current hour, ensuring that each execution of the integration flow retrieves only the latest contact records.

Step 3: Get Sales Cloud Contacts - Fetching Data from SAP Sales Cloud V2

To retrieve contact data from SAP Sales Cloud V2, we need to set up a Request Reply and connect it to an HTTP Receiver Adapter. This ensures that the iFlow sends a request to SAP Sales Cloud V2 and processes the response dynamically.

1. Setting Up the Request Reply Step

The Request Reply step is required to handle synchronous communication with SAP Sales Cloud. It ensures that the request is sent, and the response is processed before moving to the next step in the iFlow.

  1. Add a Request Reply step after the Content Modifier.
  2. Connect the Request Reply step to an HTTP Receiver Adapter.

2. Configuring the HTTP Receiver Adapter

The HTTP Receiver Adapter is responsible for sending the request to SAP Sales Cloud V2. The configuration is as follows:

Configure the connection to SAP Sales Cloud V2

  • Address: The endpoint for retrieving contact persons: 
    https://mytenant.crm.cloud.sap/sap/c4c/api/v1/contact-person-service/contactPersons
  • Method: GET is selected to fetch data.
  • Proxy Type: Set to Internet, ensuring external API communication.
  • Authentication: Basic
  • Credentials: Since SAP Sales Cloud V2 requires authentication, we must configure Basic Authentication in the Security Material section of the SAP Integration Suite.
    • Set up Basic Credentials in the SAP Integration Suite: Go to "Monitor" → "Security Material" → Create a new "User Credentials" → enter a Credential Name, Username and Password → add the Credential Name in HTTP Receiver Adapter

The dynamic filter query that was set up in the Content Modifier is passed automatically in the request to ensure only relevant contact data is retrieved.

Step 4: Extract Sales Cloud Contacts - Extracting Contact Information

In this step, we process the contact data retrieved from SAP Sales Cloud V2, extract the relevant fields, and format them for further use in the integration. To achieve this, we use a Groovy script that structures the data according to SAP Emarsys' requirements. For simplicity, this example extracts only the first name, last name, and email address, but additional fields can be included as needed.

 

 

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper;
import groovy.json.JsonOutput;

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def body = message.getBody(String)  
    def jsonSlurper = new JsonSlurper()
    def jsonResponse = jsonSlurper.parseText(body)  

    if (!jsonResponse || !jsonResponse.value || jsonResponse.value.isEmpty()) {
        throw new RuntimeException("No contact data found. API Response: " + body)
    }

    def contactsList = jsonResponse.value.collect { contact ->
        return [
            "3": contact.eMail ?: "",  
            "1": contact.givenName ?: "",  
            "2": contact.familyName ?: ""  
        ]
    }

    def emarsysPayload = [
        key_id: "3",
        contacts: contactsList
    ]

    message.setProperty("EmarsysBody", JsonOutput.toJson(emarsysPayload))  // Store entire JSON in a property

    return message;
}

 

We start by retrieving the API response as a string and parsing it into a JSON object. If no contacts are found, we throw an exception to prevent unnecessary API calls.

Next, we extract the key attributes needed for SAP Emarsys. In this example, we focus on:

  • Email Address (3)
  • First Name (1)
  • Last Name (2)

We iterate over the contact list and map each contact’s attributes into a structured format. If any of these fields are missing, we assign an empty string to ensure consistency in the data structure.

Once we have extracted the required fields, we structure the data into a JSON payload that SAP Emarsys can process. Since SAP Emarsys uses the email address as the unique identifier, we set the key_id to 3

Finally, we store the structured JSON in a message property (EmarsysBody), making it available for the next step in the iFlow.

Step 5: Configure SAP Emarsys Request

In this step, we prepare the data extracted from SAP Sales Cloud V2 for submission to the SAP Emarsys API. This involves setting up a Content Modifier and configuring the necessary OpenID Connect (OIDC, OAuth 2.0, JWT) based API authentication using the Integration Suite.

1. Add a Content Modifier

First, add a Content Modifier step in the iFlow after the Groovy script. The purpose of this step is to store the payload that will be sent to the SAP Emarsys API.

Before creating the new headers, delete the existing CamelHttpQuery header to avoid conflicts with the new request configuration. To do this, add a new row in the Message Header section and select Delete as the action. Enter CamelHttpQuery as the header name and leave the other fields empty.

Message Header

HTTP headers for Emarsys request and OAuth authentication

Next, we need to add the required headers for the OAuth 2.0 request and data exchange with the SAP Emarsys API:

  • Go to the Message Header tab.
  • Add first header:
    • Name: Content-Type
    • Source Type: Constant
    • Source Value: application/json
  • Add second header:
    • Name: Accept
    • Source Type: Constant
    • Source Value: application/json
Message Body

Next, we need to define the message body that will be sent to the SAP Emarsys API. This body contains the structured contact data extracted and formatted in the Groovy script:

  • Navigate to the Message Body tab.
  • Type: Expression 
  • Body: ${property.EmarsysBody} - we set this property in the groovy script.

2. Configure OAuth 2.0 Authentication

To connect to the SAP Emarsys API, you need to configure OAuth 2.0 authentication in the Integration Suite. This is done by setting up OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials in the Security Material.

Configuring Security Material for Emarsys Authentication

You can obtain the OAuth 2.0 credentials directly from SAP Emarsys or configure them using OpenID Connect with SAP Cloud Identity. For detailed steps on setting up credentials via OpenID Connect, refer to the official  SAP Emarsys documentation here.

Alternatively, you can create API credentials directly in SAP Emarsys. You can find the detailed steps here. Ensure that the Token Service URL is correctly set to the SAP Emarsys token endpoint.

When configuring OAuth 2.0 credentials in the Security Material of the Integration Suite, make sure to populate the SCOPE field (e.g.'api'). Missing scope values can lead to authentication errors. For more details on setting up OAuth 2.0 authentication, refer to this SAP Community blog.

Step 6: Create Contacts in SAP Emarsys

In this step, we create new contacts in SAP Emarsys using the extracted and formatted data from SAP Sales Cloud V2. To achieve this, we need to configure a Request Reply Step and an HTTP Receiver Adapter to send the contact data to SAP Emarsys.

1. Setting Up the Request Reply Step

  1. Add a Request Reply after the Content Modifier.
  2. Connect the Request Reply to an HTTP Receiver Adapter.

2. Configuring the HTTP Receiver Adapter

  • Address: The endpoint for creating contacts in SAP Emarsys: https://api.emarsys.net/api/v3/contact
  • Proxy Type: Internet
  • Method: POST
  • Authentication: OAuth2 Client Credentials
  • Credential Name: Your credential name from the previous step

At this point, the contact data extracted from SAP Sales Cloud V2 is ready to be created in SAP Emarsys. The HTTP Receiver Adapter sends the formatted data as a POST request to the SAP Emarsys API.

 

Validating the Integration

After setting up the iFlow and configuring all necessary steps, we can now test the integration to confirm that contact data is successfully transferred from SAP Sales Cloud V2 to SAP Emarsys.

Step 1: Create a Contact in SAP Sales Cloud V2

To validate the integration, we start by creating a new contact in SAP Sales Cloud V2. In this example, a contact named Emma Evans with the email address emma.evans@alpha.cx.demo.sap was created in SAP Sales Cloud V2.

Step 2: Verify That the Contact is Not Yet Available in SAP Emarsys

Next, we search for the newly created contact in SAP Emarsys using the email address. As shown in the screenshot, the search returns no results, confirming that the contact is not yet present in SAP Emarsys.

Step 3: Execute the Integration Flow

We then manually trigger the iFlow to push the newly created contact from SAP Sales Cloud V2 to SAP Emarsys. The iFlow sends a POST request to the SAP Emarsys API with the contact details extracted and formatted in the previous steps.

Step 4: Confirm That the Contact is Now Available in SAP Emarsys

Finally, we search for the contact in SAP Emarsys again using the email address. This time, the contact is successfully found, confirming that the data transfer was successful. The contact's first name, last name, and email address are correctly displayed in SAP Emarsys, indicating that the iFlow is working as expected.

 

Conclusion

In this post, we walked through the process of setting up an end-to-end integration between SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys using the Cloud Integration component within the SAP Integration Suite. We started by configuring the connection to SAP Sales Cloud V2, filtering the data to retrieve only relevant contacts, and extracting the necessary fields using a Groovy script. We then prepared the data for SAP Emarsys, set up OpenID Connect (OIDC, OAuth 2.0, JWT) based API authentication, and sent the data to SAP Emarsys using an HTTP Receiver Adapter.

The successful execution of the integration was confirmed when the contact created in SAP Sales Cloud V2 appeared in SAP Emarsys after the process ran. This demonstrates how easily the SAP Integration Suite can be used to connect SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys - laying the foundation for more complex processes in the future. 

By following these steps, you can build a similar integration in your environment, adapting the filter conditions and field mapping to meet your specific business needs.

entry.title: Advantages of Proactive SAP IDM Migration
entry.content:
Preparing for SAP IDM’s End of Maintenance: Why Proactive Action Matters

SAP Identity Management (IDM) has long been a cornerstone of many organizations' Identity and Access Management (IAM) strategies. However, with the official end of maintenance set for 2027 (and extended maintenance only available until 2030), the clock is ticking for businesses relying on this solution. It’s critical to act now and start planning your journey to transition to another IDM system. Delaying the process could lead to unforeseen risks, such as compliance violations, operational disruptions, or increased migration costs.
Proactive migration allows you to mitigate these risks while seizing the opportunity to modernize and future-proof your IAM ecosystem. With a structured approach, you can transform this necessity into a strategic advantage.

 

Re-Engineering Your IAM Processes: A Unique Opportunity

Migrating from SAP IDM isn’t just about replacing one tool with another - it’s a chance to revolutionize your approach to identity and access management. Here’s why this transition offers unparalleled potential for improvement:

  • Redefine your IAM Requirements: Take this opportunity to collect updated business needs and redefine your identity management requirements.
  • Adapt to Hybrid or Cloud-Only Landscapes: Modernize your IAM processes to meet the demands of hybrid and cloud-first environments, ensuring flexibility and scalability.
  • Overcome Current Product Limitations: Address and eliminate process constraints imposed by your SAP IDM system, unlocking new possibilities.
  • Incorporate AI Capabilities: Integrate artificial intelligence into your IAM to enhance security, streamline operations, and automate decision-making.
  • Leverage the SAP Toolchain: Utilize powerful tools like LeanIX and Signavio to gain transparency, optimize processes, and ensure smooth integration throughout your migration.

The SAP IDM migration project is your chance to align IAM processes with current and future business strategies, ensuring your organization stays ahead in an ever-evolving digital landscape.

SAP IAM Reference Architecture (Source: SAP Discovery Center)

 

Expert Support for a Successful Transition

SAP Professional Services offers tailored guidance to help you migrate from SAP IDM effectively - the SAP IDM Migration Coach. Our offerings includes:

  • Expert coaching to navigate your migration journey with confidence.
  • Support for seamless integration of your next IAM solution.
  • Collaboration with Microsoft for Entra ID-based migrations.

Business outcomes with SAP Professional Services, you’ll achieve:

  • A smooth migration process, ready for future integration and compliance needs.
  • AI-compatible, future-resilient IAM systems built for the cloud.
  • Enhanced transparency and optimized user management using SAP tools like Signavio and LeanIX.

 

Get Started Today

Ready to embark on your SAP IDM migration journey? Find out more about the offering in SAP IDM Migration Coach - One Pager  and get in contact with SAP Professional Services today to learn more about how our expert team can assist you in achieving a seamless and successful transition. Reach out to us and take the first step towards a future-ready Identity Management System.

 

Related Topics

Preparing for SAP Identity Management’s End-of-Maintenance in 2027 (blog)

Migrate identity management scenarios from SAP IDM to Microsoft Entra (learning) 

 

__PRESENT

entry.title: SAP Cloud Identity Services - New Features updates for 11th March 2025
entry.content:
Cloud Identity Services - Authorizations Based on Policies
You can now restrict the access to the administration console only to one user type via the new attribute that is supported for authorizations based on policies - user.type.
Reference Documentation ; Configure User Authorizations
 
Cloud Identity Services - Identity Federation for Applications
You can enable identity federation for an application to override the identity federation settings on the configured corporate identity provider for the application. 

Choose if user attributes will be taken from the corporate IdP assertion or from Identity Authentication user store. Restrict access based on user profile.

Cloud Identity Services - SAML 2.0 Configuration

The SAML 2.0 configuration page on application level has been refactored from March 11th 2025 release. Now you can separately configure the metadata and certificates setting

 
Cloud Identity Services - Attributes Based on Flexible Expressions
The display name of Groups attribute based on flexible expressions for the application is renamed to All Groups.
Reference Documentation : Configure Attributes Based on Flexible Expressions

entry.title: SAP Ariba User Provisioning Using SAP Identity Provisioning Service (IPS)
entry.content:
This blog post explains how to provision users to SAP Ariba using the SAP Cloud Identity Services—Identity Provisioning Service (IPS). Typically, you can create and provision new users to SAP Ariba through various methods such as: 

  • Self-registration
  • CSV file upload
  • Provisioning via IPS from existing user stores (e.g., Microsoft AD, AS ABAP, SAP SuccessFactors)

More information about Integrating SAP Ariba with SAP Cloud Identity Services can be found on SAP Help. While there are multiple options, this post focuses on using SAP Identity Provisioning to provision users from SAP Cloud Identity Service (Identity Authentication).

Note: For guidance on integrating Cloud Identity Services with SAP Ariba (for Single Sign-On and identity federation), see the blog post here:
Identity Federation & SAP Ariba SSO with SAP Cloud Identity Services

Use-Case Scenario

Integrating Cloud Identity Services with SAP Ariba is just one example of how enterprises can unify identity management across various SAP solutions (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Business Network, SAP Category Management, SAP Fieldglass). Centralizing identities in SAP Cloud Identity Service is an essential part of an enterprise-wide identity strategy. It also aligns with future SAP initiatives such as SAP Task Center and Joule, which rely on users being managed in Cloud Identity Service.

Identity Authentication and Identity Provisioning play a critical role in integrating SAP Ariba and other SAP BTP applications. They enable the use of a Global User ID—an attribute that uniquely identifies a user across the system landscape—serving as a prerequisite for adopting tools like SAP Task Center.

Architecture Overview

The architecture diagram for this scenario primarily highlights how to provision identities to SAP Ariba via Identity Provisioning. Similar concepts can also apply to other SAP Ariba SaaS solutions such as SAP Business Network, SAP Category Management, and SAP Fieldglass.

In this blog, we will cover:

  1. Creating Ariba users in SAP Cloud Identity Service using both ODM and a custom Ariba schema.
  2. Setting up SAP Ariba as the target system and Identity Authentication as the source system.
  3. Configuring the IPS transformation file to map Identity Authentication user attributes to SAP Ariba user attributes.
  4. Configuring IPS jobs to provision users to SAP Ariba.
  5. Planning the migration from the custom Ariba Cloud Identity Service schema to the standard SAP Ariba schema (once released).

Create Ariba Users in SAP Cloud Identity Service Using ODM and a Custom Ariba Schema

Beyond the standard/core user attributes in SAP Cloud Identity Service, SAP Ariba requires additional attributes (such as purchasing unit, purchasing organization, plant, etc.). To handle these, two extension schemas come into play:

  • ODM Schema: urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap.odm:2.0:User
  • SAP Ariba Schema: urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:ariba:2.0:User

SAP Cloud Identity Service has recently released Open Domain Model (ODM) schema support, enabling additional business attributes (e.g., companyCode, purchasingOrganization) in the Cloud Identity Service user profile. The ODM schema and attributes can be found under Users & Authentication → Schemas in your Cloud Identity Service.

Note: As of this writing, the SAP Ariba Schema (urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:ariba:2.0:User) is planned for release in Q2 2025 (subject to change based on the SAP Cloud Identity Service product roadmap). In the interim, a custom schema must be created, which you will later migrate to the standard SAP Ariba schema once it becomes available.

Steps to Create a Custom SAP Ariba Schema

You can create the custom schema either via Postman or directly in the Cloud Identity Service interface. Below are the steps for creating it through the Cloud Identity Service UI:

  1. Navigate to Schemas
    • In the Cloud Identity Service console, go to Users & Authorizations → Schemas.
  2. Create New Schema
    • Click Create or Add, and enter a name for your custom schema. 
    • Important: The custom schema name (urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:vendor:ariba:2.0:User) must be different from the future standard schema name (urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:ariba:2.0:User) to avoid conflicts once the standard schema is released. 
  3. Define Schema Attributes
    • Add the additional attributes (e.g., currency, deliverTo, passwordAdapter, addresses). 

Create an Ariba User Using ODM and the Custom Ariba Schema in Cloud Identity Service

  1. Create a System User
    • In the Cloud Identity Service console, go to Users & Authorizations → Administrators → Add System.
    • Generate the Client ID and Client Secret, ensuring you have Manage Users permissions.

2. Prepare a User Payload in Postman

    • Use a sample JSON payload that includes ODM attributes and the custom Ariba attributes.
    • Ensure any attributes values you include must match valid SAP Ariba fields values so that the data correctly populates in SAP Ariba

3. Verify User Creation

    • After sending the request to create a user, check the Cloud Identity Service to confirm the new user.
    • The Extensions tab in the user record should display both ODM and custom Ariba attributes.

4. Assign the User to a Group

    • In Cloud Identity Service, assign the user to any necessary group(s) (for ex: ARIBA-USERS group) to manage their access permissions.

       

This completes the initial step of creating an Ariba user in Cloud Identity Service with both ODM and custom Ariba schema attributes.

Set Up Identity Authentication as the Source System and SAP Ariba as the Target System in IPS

Identity Authentication as the Source System

  1. Navigate to Identity Provisioning in your Cloud Identity Service console.
  2. Create a Source System and select Identity Authentication as the source type.
  3. Modify default transformation file to map ODM and custom Ariba schema attributes.
    • Download sample source transformation file from Github .
    • Note that the schema references in the transformation file should point to the ODM attributes and your custom Ariba schema attributes.

       

    • In the Source System Properties, you can set filters like ias.group.filter or ias.user.filter so that only specific users (e.g., aribacisuser1) are provisioned. 

SAP Ariba as the Target System

  1. In Identity Provisioning, navigate to Target Systems and create a new system of type SAP Ariba Application.

     

  2. SAP Ariba user management is performed via SCIM APIs. Follow the SAP Help documentation to request access to the SAP Ariba SCIM API, after which you will receive OAuth client credentials.
  3. Download and adjust the Target System Transformation File
    • Map your custom Ariba schema attributes to the standard SAP Ariba attributes.
    • For instance, ODM schema attributes can map to Accounting attributes in Ariba, while custom schema attributes might map to Shipping and Billing details.

Configure IPS Jobs to Provision Users to SAP Ariba

Once your source and target systems are set up:

  1. Run the Provisioning Job in IPS.
  2. The user you filtered (e.g., aribacisuser1) should be created in the SAP Ariba target system.
  3. In the Ariba system, navigate to Manage → Core Administration → User Manager. Search for the user.
  4. Additional business attributes (such as purchasing org, cost centers) appear under Other User Info in Ariba.

Note: As mentioned, ODM schema attributes are mapped to Accounting attributes, and custom schema (SAP:Ariba) attributes are mapped to Shipping and Billing attributes.

 

Custom Ariba Cloud Identity Service Schema Migration Plan

As of this writing, the standard SAP Ariba schema (urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:ariba:2.0:User) is not yet available. Once SAP releases it (currently planned for Q2 2025(subject to change based on the SAP Cloud Identity Service product roadmap)), follow these steps to migrate from your custom schema:

  1. Update Existing Users
    • Perform an Update operation on all Ariba users in Cloud Identity Service, replacing the custom schema (urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:vendor:ariba:2.0:User) with the standard schema (urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:ariba:2.0:User).
  2. Populate Attributes
    • Confirm that attributes are correctly mapped and populated in the new standard schema.
  3. Adjust Source and Target Transformation Files
    • Replace all references to the custom schema with the standard Ariba schema.
  4. Run IPS Jobs
    • Execute your IPS provisioning jobs again to ensure the updated schema references are correct and that data syncs as expected.

 

Conclusion

By following the steps outlined in this blog, you can successfully create and manage SAP Ariba users through SAP Cloud Identity Service, leveraging both the ODM schema and a custom Ariba schema (until the standard one is released). Identity Provisioning Service streamlines the process, allowing you to filter and transform user data from Identity Authentication into SAP Ariba. Once SAP releases the standard SAP Ariba schema, you can seamlessly migrate your custom Ariba schema, further simplifying your identity landscape and supporting broader initiatives like SAP Task Center and Joule.

entry.title: SSO implementation in GROW with SAP using Microsoft Entra ID
entry.content:
The SAP Single Sign-On application enables users to log in once to gain secure access to all the software they require throughout the day with no need to log in again ,with SAP Single Sign-On

There are several process to set up setup SSO in On premise system as well as on cloud, here we will discuss SSO setup between Azure and SAP on Public Cloud using IdP

There are two primary options in which SAP Identity Authentication Services and Microsoft Entra ID can be integrated:

  • Microsoft Entra ID as the Identity Provider (IdP): This scenario makes Microsoft Entra ID the central authentication hub, with users logging into SAP applications using their Microsoft Entra ID credentials.

 

  • SAP IAS as the IdP: In this case, SAP IAS becomes the primary authentication source, with users logging into Microsoft Entra ID applications using their SAP credentials.

Recently I got opportunity to setup SSO between Microsoft Entra ID (formerly known as Microsoft Azure Active Directory or Azure AD) , SAP IAS and SuccessFactors on SAP on Public Cloud (Grow with SAP) using SAP IAS as the IdP.

 

Below are step to configure SSO between Azure ,IAS and Success Factor.

1- Configure SAP IAS

Login on IAS system go to Applications and Resources -> Tenant Settings ->Single Sign On-> SAML 2.0 Configuration the  download metadata file on you system share this file to Azure team and ask for Federation Metadata XML 

 

 

 


2- Configure Microsoft Entra ID

This setp should be perform by azure admin system by following step

Create an Application in Microsoft Entra ID: This application represents your SAP IAS instance. Login to https://portal.azure.com and setup the Microsoft Entra ID.

 



Click Add -> Enterprise Applications 



  •  

 


By default, Microsoft Azure supports variety of applications. Search with SAP Cloud Identity Services. Select the SAP Cloud Identity Services and click on create.

 


We will be using the SAML Metadata file to setup the trust between Microsoft Entra ID and SAP Identity Authentication service (IAS). Click on Setup Single Sign-On.

 


 

Choose SAML as the SSO method and upload the SAP IAS metadata file.

 



 


After saving the application you can download the Federation Metadata XML file which we will add to the SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS).

 

3- Configure Federation metadata on  IAS

Login on IAS system and click identity Provider  and select Corporate identity Provider

 

Click on create

 

 

Fill are require data

 

 

Click on create it will create entry in Identity provider

 

 

 Now click on SAML2.0 Configuration

 

And upload azure xml file

 

 

 

 

All setting between Azure and IAS system has been done.You can check  SSO connection via login on IAS system ,it will pick Azure authentication and will login without asking password on IAS system

Now going to setup SSO connection between IAS and managed application system here I will setup connection between IAS to success factor, you can choose other system depend on requirement

Login on IAS system and click on Application & Resources and select SuccessFactors system

 

Now  select single Sign-on  and check Subject Name Identifier it should be as below

 

 

 

 

select single Sign-on  and check Default Name ID Format

 

 

Check attribute it should be as below

 

 

Now maintain domain in Conditional Authentication as per below

 

 

 

All setting has been completed for SF system

 

 

 

 

Now login to sf provision url and enable SSO

 

 

 

 

Now test sso from SF system it will automatically login on system without login page

 

Reference

  • Simplify SSO with Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) & ... - SAP Community
  • https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/saas-apps/sap-hana-cloud-platform-identity-authentication-tutorial

entry.title: AI Innovation Starts Here: Join the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Today
entry.content:
Ready to harness the power of AI and transform your industry? With SAP’s advanced AI technology, we help you optimize operations, enhance decision-making, and drive efficiency across your business.

Now’s your chance to influence the next wave of AI innovation. Join our SAP Customer Engagement Initiative projects, where your feedback will directly shape the AI solutions we develop to meet your unique needs. Your insights will be instrumental in creating tools that help you achieve real, measurable success.

Don’t miss this opportunity to lead the way in digital transformation with AI. Register for the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative today – registration closes on March 28, 2025!

  • BTP AI based Data Anonymization Service
  • Create AI-driven extensions and automations with SAP Build
  • Improving the Admin's and Solution Architect's Experience on SAP BTP
  • Insights into Business processes with Business Event Logging
  • Joule and GenAI use cases in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud
  • OData UI Service from scratch Generator
  • Research into AI-Assisted User Learning Rollout for Adoption
  • Sales and Use Tax Reporting in United States - User Research
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Provisioning, Integration with Joule 
  • SAP Concur - AI Managed Expense Reports
  • SAP CX AI Toolkit
  • SAP Integration Suite
  • The AI-Driven Future of New Product Introduction (NPI)

We believe that collaboration is key to unlocking innovation. By joining forces with our customers and partners, we can collectively shape the future of SAP’s products and change the way businesses operate using AI.

Seize this opportunity – register on influence.sap.com and be a part of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 1 in 2025! If you are also interested in other SAP Customer Engagement Initiative projects that are currently open for registration, check out the full list here.

Warm regards, 
Your SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Team 

Follow-up after registration: When you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call with the SAP project lead. At this point further participation is optional. Typically, all activities are governed by the Feedback Agreement with SAP.

entry.title: SAP IAG intergration with SAP Analytical cloud (SAC)
entry.content:
SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance (IAG) serves as a pivotal cloud-based solution, orchestrating identity and access management across intricate hybrid environments. By centralizing access request processes through a user-friendly, self-service portal, IAG streamlines provisioning for both on-premises and cloud applications, including seamless integration with SAP Analytics Cloud.

The first step is configuring SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance (IAG) within SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) environment involves establishing a proxy destination. Specifically, the creation of the IPS_PROXY destination within your subscriber subaccount is paramount for seamless communication and functionality.

 

Step 1. Create Proxy Destination in BTP is not created.

  1. Navigate to the subscriber subaccount for SAP Cloud Identity Access governance in SAP BTP and create a destination with the name IPS_PROXY as shown in the table below.
  2. Enter the Properties listed in the table below for the destination. All properties must be entered. Some properties must be added as Additional Properties. Copy the names of all properties as displayed. Property names and values are case sensitive.

    The url in the above can be fetched from CIS cockpit. 

    For user and password

    1. Go to the Identity Authentication, Users Authorizations-> Administrator and enable the option Manage Identity Provisioning for your user.
    2. Open your Identity Provisioning and navigate to Security Authorizations   Manage User Authorizations -> Administrators.
    3. In the section Users & Authorizations Administrators, Add System for Identity Provisioning and Configure Authorizations for the Access Proxy System API. Note down the Client ID and Secret (once the secret is generated, you cannot retrieve or change it.).

       

      Step 2:Create Proxy system for SAC in CIS.

      Maintain the properties exactly as shown:

      The OAuthtoken, user, URL and password is to be fetched from SAC.

      In SAC: Goto system-> Administrator

      Step 3: Create application in IAG.

      Run Repository sync. 

       

      This completes the SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance (IAG) integration with SAP Analytics Cloud, enabling centralized, secure access management

Regards
JP

entry.title: Integrating SAP Cloud Identity Services (Identity Authentication - IAS) with SAP Audit Log Viewer
entry.content:
Previous Blog : https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/how-to-enable-sap-audit-log-viewer-service-to-collect-all-audit-logs-from/ba-p/14037435 

Accessing Audit Logs for Identity Authentication Tenants

To access the audit logs for changes in personal data, successful, and failed authentications for Identity Authentication tenants on both SAP, AWS, and Azure infrastructures, you can use the Audit Log Service in SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry. This process is essential for organizations that require detailed auditing to ensure that changes are tracked accurately.

Why is this Important?

Auditing is crucial for maintaining the integrity and security of your organization's data. By tracking who made changes, you can ensure accountability and transparency within your system.

Steps to Access Audit Logs

  1. Navigate to the Audit Log Service: Access the Audit Log Service in SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry.
  2. Filter Logs: Use filters to view logs related to changes in personal data, successful authentications, and failed authentications.
  3. Review and Analyze: Carefully review the logs to identify any unauthorized changes or any suspicious activities.

Benefits

  • Enhanced Security: By monitoring audit logs, you can detect and respond to security breaches promptly.
  • Compliance: Ensure your organization meets regulatory requirements for data protection and privacy.
  • Accountability: Track changes to identify responsible parties and maintain accountability

Prerequisites for enabling Auditlogs Viewer for SAP Identity Authentication - Audit logs

Pick your subaccount details and you must have SAP Cloud Identity Services and Auditlog Viewer Services enabled already.

Once above details are available, you can now follow the below steps to tie the application between SAP Cloud Identity Services and Auditlog Viewer Services

Step 1 : Login to your SAP Cloud Identity Services - Admin and go to Monitoring & Reporting Tile

Step 2 : Click Add to update configuration of your SAP BTP Tenant details (from above prerequisites steps)

Step 3 : Update your BTP Tenant Id and Subdomain which should match to the region shown in dropdown. List of available regions are in below link.List of Available Regions for Identity Authentication - Cloud Foundry Regions Mapping

Step 4: Once configured,you will be able to see the Subaccount Information and Auditlog viewer Link and you will have to wait for 15-20mins once configured to see the logs in Auditlog Viewer Portal.

Step 5 : Upon accessing the Audit Log Viewer, you have the option to filter the logs based on date and keyword filters.

The audit logs provide information about the event category and timestamp, the event and object type, who performed the action and others.

entry.title: Identity Federation: Substitution of Subject Name Identifier in Identity Authentication
entry.content:
Identity Federation: Substitution of Subject Name Identifier in Identity Authentication  

 Context

Existing Identity Authentication service customers is sending a SubjectNameIdentifier as part of SAML response (say EmailID for SAP FieldGlass) from their Identity Provider (IdP). To onboard another SAP Cloud Application (say SAP Ariba, which mostly needs EmployeeID ) a different SubjectNameIdentifier is needed.

We have a customer who informed that they cannot create another IdP instance because multiple IdPs against a single SAP Cloud Identity Service tenant (  https://<IAS tenant  id>-accounts.ondemand.com ) is not allowed as it's designed to be a single, centralized authentication and authorization service

Solution:

Leverage attribute section of SAML Response to pass the SubjectNameIdentifier which will be substituted within IAS application.

Step 1:
Identify the attribute that will serve as the NameID for the second SAP Cloud Application (Service Provider <SP >). Ensure this attribute is included in the Corporate IdP’s SAML response within the additional attributes section. 

Step 2:
Configure the corresponding IAS application for the second SP (SAP Ariba) so that it substitutes the SubjectNameIdentifier with the chosen additional attribute.

 

Here “IdP Proxy” is the IAS Application which is substituting the NameId

 

Configurations: 

Config changes to be made within IAS Application corresponding to the new SP  (SAP Ariba) are below: 

Prerequisites:

Configuration: SAP Ariba SSO with SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication

Identity Federation: SAP Ariba SSO with SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication

 

  • Use "Identity Authentication user store” = ON < Although User Data is not picked up from SAP Cloud Identity Serices User store, this is needed >

(Path: Home > Corporate Identity Providers> select the IDP> Identity Federation > Enable “Use Identity Authentication user store”)

 

  • Subject Name Identifier > Primary Attribute

Option 1: Standard Attribute
Source = Corporate Identity Provider
Value = lastName

Option 2: Custom Attribute
Source = expression
Value = ${corporateIdP.attributeName}

  ( Path : Home> Application & Resources > Application >    (Select the Application > Subject Name Identifier)

 

We configured the above explained setup in SAP Ariba Sandbox realm with trial BTP Account IAS Tenant and used mock IdP Service. 

Below are the SAML 2.0 Responses captured during the POC run:

In SAML 2.0 Assertion, Attribute NameID holds the SubjectNameIdentifier value. 

i) SAML Response from Corporate Identity Provider ( IdP )  to SAP Cloud Identity Service Tenant  -  Attribute Name "lastName" = "sghosh01"  and NameID = sghosh01@example.com
ii) Within SAP Cloud Identity Service, the IAS Application substitutes NameID from "sghosh01@example.com" to "sghosh01" (the value from lastName Attribute) and sends the SAML to SAP Ariba ( SP) 

In real scenarios instead of lastName Attribute another attribute like LoginName should be used to hold the Ariba User UniqueName.

Corporate IdP to SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant:

Here in SAML Tracer Summary Tab above,  Subject shows the NameID attribute value.

Actual SAML 2.0 Assertion snippet showing /Response/Assertion/Subject/NameID 

Full SAML 2.0 Response from Corporate IdP to SAP Cloud Identity Service here

<samlp:Response xmlns:samlp="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:protocol"
                xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"
                Destination="https://a4fap5hd3.trial-accounts.ondemand.com/saml2/idp/acs/a4fap5hd3.trial-accounts.ondemand.com"
                ID="_beaf567bc4576dbbee1a"
                InResponseTo="S9ad1e6b1-c067-4b3d-9951-e9274a4870cd"
                IssueInstant="2025-02-25T15:18:37.663Z"
                Version="2.0"
                >
    <saml:Issuer xmlns:saml="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:assertion"
                 Format="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:nameid-format:entity"
                 >https://saml.example.com/entityid</saml:Issuer>

        <SignedInfo>
            <CanonicalizationMethod Algorithm="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/xml-exc-c14n#" />
            <SignatureMethod Algorithm="http://www.w3.org/2001/04/xmldsig-more#rsa-sha256" />

                <Transforms>
                    <Transform Algorithm="http://www.w3.org/2000/09/xmldsig#enveloped-signature" />

                </Transforms>

            </Reference>
        </SignedInfo>
        <SignatureValue></SignatureValue>
        <KeyInfo>
            <X509Data>
                <X509Certificate>MIIC4jCCAcoCCQC33wnybT5QZDANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQsFADAyMQswCQYDVQQGEwJV
SzEPMA0GA1UECgwGQm94eUhRMRIwEAYDVQQDDAlNb2NrIFNBTUwwIBcNMjIwMjI4
